rationale: This example demonstrates the core transformation of the Rubottom oxidation: the conversion of a silyl enol ether to an α-hydroxy ketone. The substrate is the trimethylsilyl enol ether of acetone, one of the simplest possible silyl enol ethers. The product is hydroxyacetone (α-hydroxypropanone). m-Chloroperoxybenzoic acid (mCPBA) is a classic peracid reagent used for this reaction. The reaction is simple, acyclic, and clearly illustrates the α-hydroxylation of a carbonyl compound via its silyl enol ether derivative, which is the defining feature of the Rubottom oxidation.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses a simple acyclic ketone-derived silyl enol ether to avoid overlap with more complex natural product syntheses or other named reactions involving cyclic systems (e.g., Baeyer-Villiger oxidation, which also uses peracids but on ketones). The substrate is specific to silyl enol ethers, distinguishing it from general alkene epoxidation reactions. The product is a simple α-hydroxy ketone, avoiding motifs that could trigger other named reaction queries.
